lesson_title: How to design your first programs

cards.explanation:
Follow this process:
  • 1. Define the problem: What should the program do?
  • 2. Design the solution: How will it work?
  • 3. Break into subtasks: Identify functions needed
  • 4. Implement: Write code incrementally
  • 5. Test: Verify each part works
  • 6. Iterate: Refine and improve
examples: // Solution: Get inputs, sum them, divide

cards.back: Top-down design starts with the big picture (main problem) and breaks it down into smaller, more manageable sub-problems/functions.
Incremental development means building your program one small piece at a time, testing each piece before moving on to the next.

cards.text: Write programs {{c1::incrementally}}, adding and testing {{c2::one feature}} at a time, rather than writing everything at once.

cards.front: How do you know when to create a new function?
cards.back:
Create a function when:
  • Code is used multiple times
  • A task is logically distinct
  • Code is complex and needs organization
  • You want to test something independently

cards.front: Why should you test code frequently during development?
cards.back:
Frequent testing:
  • Catches bugs early when they're easier to fix
  • Ensures each piece works before adding complexity
  • Makes debugging easier (smaller scope)
  • Builds confidence in the code

cards.concept: Understanding data flow in program design
cards.explanation:
When designing functions, consider:
  • Input: What data does the function need? (parameters)
  • Processing: What does it do with the data?
  • Output: What does it produce? (return value)
Each function should have a clear purpose in the data flow.
examples: int square(int x);         // Input: x, Output: x²

cards.front: What should you do before writing code?
cards.back:
Before coding:
  • Understand the problem fully
  • Plan your approach
  • Sketch out the main functions
  • Consider edge cases
  • Think about data flow
Planning saves time later.

cards.front: What should the main() function typically do?
cards.back: main() should coordinate the program's flow by calling other functions to do the actual work. Keep main() simple and high-level.

cards.front: What is pseudocode and when is it useful?
cards.back: Pseudocode is a plain-language description of what code should do. It's useful for planning logic before writing actual C++ code, helping organize thoughts without worrying about syntax.
